text: The surgeon mobilizes the left lobe of the liver and dissects the liver hilum, identifying a large hilar cyst and a dilated distal bile duct within the gallbladder.
The surgeon isolates and divides the common bile duct, preparing to remove the distal bile duct.
The surgeon uses cephalic traction with the third arm to carefully dissect the intrapancreatic portion of the bile duct down to its union with the pancreatic duct.
The surgeon clips and resects the root of the bile duct, ensuring the main pancreatic duct remains intact.
The surgeon clamps the left hepatic artery at the hilum, resulting in visible discoloration of the left lobe of the liver.
The surgeon resects the left hepatic artery and isolates the left portal vein, with visible manipulation of these vascular structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses wrist-leg movements of the working instrument to achieve vascular control, focusing on the caudate branch of the portal vein.
The surgeon performs a cholecystectomy, removing the distended gallbladder to prevent interference with the operative field during the hepatectomy procedure.
The surgeon isolates the right anterior hepatic duct, which drains into a large hilar cyst, using precise dissection techniques.
Firefly fluorescent imaging is used to enhance the visualization of the biliary structures, highlighting them with a distinct fluorescence for precise identification.
The surgeon temporarily ligates the isolated right anterior hepatic duct to prevent bile spillage, ensuring a clear operative field for the subsequent steps.
The surgeon attempts to isolate the right posterior hepatic duct, taking care to avoid vascular injury during the dissection.
Therefore, its dissection was put off until hepatic parenchymal resection.
The surgeon performs parenchymal resection using the rubber band retraction method, visibly manipulating the liver tissue to facilitate the resection process.
The surgeon sutures both edges of the hepatic resection plane with rubber bands, securing them through the abdominal wall on both sides.
During the parenchymal resection, the resection plane visibly widens due to the elastic tension of the rubber bands sutured to the edges, facilitating the surgical process.
The surgeon performs hepatic parenchymal resection using ultrasonic shears and a bipolar coagulator, carefully progressing through the tissue to minimize bleeding and ensure precise dissection.
At the hilar level, the surgeon isolates and resects the right posterior hepatic duct, ensuring safe separation from surrounding structures.
The surgeon completes the parenchymal transaction by resecting the caudate lobe and left hepatic vein, finalizing the separation of these structures from the liver.
The surgeon transects the first portion of the jejunum using an endo-GIA stapler and pulls it out through the mesocolon, preparing for hepaticojejunostomy.
The surgeon prepares the two openings of the hepatic ducts using a short silicon catheter, performing ductoplasty to create a single common opening.
The surgical team utilizes a magnified stable operative view and articulating instruments to perform a delicate procedure, ensuring precise manipulation within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a hepaticojejunostomy by applying a continuous suture to connect the hepatic duct to the jejunum.
The surgeon completes the jejuno-jejunostomy using intracorporeal suturing, connecting two segments of the jejunum with precise stitching.
The surgeon delivers the resected specimen through a fendant steel skin incision using a plastic bag, completing the hepatectomy procedure.
